nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and still floodplain waters, flooded forest (igapó and várzea), lake margins, and sheltered river backwaters of the central and western Amazon Basin; typically associated with submerged roots, woody debris, and leaf litter in soft, blackwater or clearwater environments

decorPreferences: large pieces of driftwood (roots, branches) as shelter and territory markers
subdued lighting with floating plants (Amazon frogbit, water lettuce) or tall background plants (Amazon swords, vallisneria)
dark substrate (fine dark sand or fine gravel) to reduce stress and enhance coloration
leaf litter (Indian almond / Catappa leaves) to lower pH and release tannins
minimal flow — gentle circulation only; avoid strong currents
open mid-water swimming space central to the tank

diet.notes: Wild discus are primarily carnivorous, feeding on invertebrates, insect larvae, and small crustaceans. In the aquarium, high-protein foods are essential. Beefheart-based frozen or fresh mixes have long been the traditional staple, and many experienced keepers continue to use a beefheart mix as the dietary foundation. Frozen bloodworms, frozen brine shrimp, frozen mysis shrimp, and high-quality discus-specific pellets and granules are all suitable options. Live foods such as earthworms and blackworms are accepted and beneficial for conditioning breeding pairs. Offer two to three small portions daily and remove any uneaten food promptly, as discus are particularly sensitive to the water quality decline that decomposing food causes. Farm-raised specimens generally adapt more readily to prepared foods than wild-caught individuals.

goodWith: other discus (conspecifics — kept in groups of 6+)
cardinal tetras (Paracheirodon axelrodi) — classic biotope companion
corydoras catfish (C. sterbai preferred — higher temperature tolerance)
German blue rams (Mikrogeophagus ramirezi — compatible temperature)
peaceful South American tetras tolerant of warm soft water
avoidWith: aggressive or boisterous fish that will stress discus
fin-nipping species (tiger barbs, some serpae tetras)
common plecos (disruptive, outcompete for food at night)
small shrimp during spawning (parents become very territorial)

imageCandidates.notes: Patrick Farrelly. 2405x2010. Tack-sharp lateral view of a vivid blue/turquoise wild-form Common Discus showing the classic round disc body, wavy turquoise vermiculation and red eye; whole fish in frame, well lit against green plants.
H. Zell. 5130x3983. Sharp full-body shot of a brown/orange wild-type form with blue striations and faint vertical bars; represents the natural brown/blue coloration described for the species.

Bernard DUPONT. 4000x3000. Striking red/turquoise checkerboard captive strain, sharp and vividly coloured, whole fish in frame; illustrates the selectively developed colour morphs common in the trade.

summary: The Common Discus is one of the most iconic freshwater aquarium fish, prized for its round, disc-shaped body and vivid coloration found across brown, blue, and green wild forms. Keeping it well requires warm, soft, acidic water and rigorous water quality management, making it a species best suited to advanced hobbyists.
careNotes:
The Common Discus takes in the brown, blue, and green wild color forms of the species Symphysodon aequifasciatus. A 2006 taxonomic revision by Schultz formally split the genus into three species — S. aequifasciatus (Common Discus), S. discus (Heckel Discus), and S. tarzoo (Red Discus) — though the vast majority of discus available in the aquarium trade today are captive-bred strains derived primarily from S. aequifasciatus, now available in dozens of selectively developed color morphs.

Water quality is the defining challenge of discus keeping. Discus are extremely sensitive to ammonia, nitrite, and elevated nitrate; nitrate should ideally be kept below 20 ppm and preferably under 10 ppm. Frequent partial water changes — 30–50% twice weekly, or daily in breeding setups — are considered essential by most experienced keepers. Wild fish demand very soft, acidic, warm blackwater (pH 5.5–6.5, gH 1–4, 29–31°C), but most farm-raised stock have been acclimated to slightly higher pH (6.8–7.0) and marginally cooler temperatures (27–29°C) for ease of commercial production. This divergence matters when sourcing fish: confirm the farm's parameters before purchase and acclimate new arrivals gradually. Temperature must remain stable and warm; values below 27°C are known to stress discus and render them susceptible to hole-in-the-head disease (HITH) and Hexamita infections.

Groups of at least six are recommended to distribute any social aggression and allow natural pair formation. Tank footprint matters more than volume alone — a minimum base of 120 × 50 cm is generally advised. Because discus are slow, deliberate feeders, they should not be kept with fast or boisterous species that will outcompete them at feeding time. Cardinal tetras, which share similar temperature requirements, are the classic companion choice. Corydoras sterbai is the preferred bottom-dwelling companion because it tolerates the higher temperatures discus require better than most other corydoras species.

Wild-caught specimens require strict blackwater conditions — pH 5.5–6.5, very soft water, and a tannin-rich environment — and are significantly harder to maintain and breed than farm-raised fish. Farm-raised individuals are generally hardier and more forgiving of parameters that fall slightly outside the ideal range, making them the more practical starting point for most hobbyists.
breedingNotes:
Discus are biparental substrate spawners with a reproductive strategy that sets them apart from virtually all other aquarium fish: both parents secrete a nutritious skin mucus — sometimes called 'discus milk' — that newly hatched fry feed on exclusively for the first two to three weeks of life. This obligate dependence on parental skin secretions is extremely rare among fishes and is the defining trait of the genus Symphysodon. After that initial period, fry can be transitioned to micro-foods such as newly hatched brine shrimp and micro-worms.

Successful breeding requires soft, acidic, very warm water (pH 5.5–6.5, gH 1–4, 30–31°C) and a broad, flat spawning surface — a clay pot, a large flat leaf such as an Amazon sword leaf, or a vertical spawning cone are all commonly used. Pairs form naturally from a group and should be confirmed as a bonded pair before isolation, since forced pairing is rarely productive. Clutches typically number 100–350 eggs; both parents fan and guard the eggs through a 48–60 hour incubation period. Once the wrigglers hatch, the parents move them to a pit dug in the substrate; when the fry become free-swimming, they immediately begin grazing on the parents' skin secretions and should not be separated from the parents for at least the first 10–14 days.

First-time pairs often consume their eggs, but success tends to improve with repeated spawning attempts. A dedicated breeding tank — bare-bottomed, 200 litres or larger, with minimal decor — and daily water changes of 20–30% using soft, conditioned water are broadly recommended. Breeding difficulty is rated advanced, owing to the strict water parameter requirements, the fry's obligate dependence on parental mucus, and the time needed to establish a compatible, willing pair.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Symphysodon aequifasciatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, temperature range (28–31°C), pH (5.5–7.0), hardness, native range (Amazon basin floodplains), biotope, feeding notes, breeding behaviour including parental mucus feeding, taxonomy per 2006 Schultz revision.

additional.name: FishBase — Symphysodon aequifasciatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 20 cm TL), temperature range, native range (central Amazon, Ucayali–Solimões drainages), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), diet classification. Confirmed three-species taxonomy following Schultz (2004/2006).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Discus Fish Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-oriented care guide. Provided tank size guidance (55+ gal / ~208 L for a small group, larger for breeding), temperature 82–88°F (28–31°C), pH 6.0–7.0, feeding recommendations (beefheart mix, frozen bloodworms, discus pellets), careLevel advanced, companion species (cardinal tetras, corydoras sterbai). Minimum 280 L chosen to accommodate a group of 6 adults.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Discus species profile
additional.notes: UK hobby publication. Confirmed soft acidic water requirements, farm-raised vs wild-caught parameter divergence, parental mucus fry feeding biology, group size recommendation (6+), and HITH disease susceptibility below 27°C.

additional.name: Schultz, N. (2004) — Four new species of discus (Symphysodon) with a redescription of Symphysodon discus Heckel
additional.notes: Peer-reviewed taxonomic revision establishing the three currently accepted Symphysodon species: S. aequifasciatus (Common/Blue/Brown/Green Discus), S. discus (Heckel Discus), S. tarzoo (Red/Tefe Discus). Published in Zootaxa 490. This is the authority for the current three-species classification referenced in the care notes.

fish.breedingNotes: Biparental substrate spawner. Fry depend exclusively on parental skin-mucus secretions ('discus milk') for the first two to three weeks after hatching, making parental contact obligate during this period. Clutches typically run 100–350 eggs, with incubation around 50 hours and fry becoming free-swimming after roughly three days. Soft, acidic, warm water (pH 5.5–6.5, 30–31°C) is required. Pairs form naturally from groups; first spawning attempts frequently fail, but success generally improves with subsequent tries. A bare-bottom dedicated breeding tank of 200 litres or more with daily large water changes is strongly recommended.
